title: Google Antigravity 2.0 Beginner's Guide
summary: Google Antigravity 2.0 is a standalone desktop application designed for agent-optimized workflows. It features multi-agent teams, scheduled tasks, native voice capabilities, and integration with other Google products.
writeup: Anti-Gravity 2.0 is a standalone desktop app for orchestrating AI coding agents, built on Gemini models, demonstrated here by building a street-guessing map game called Street Guesser from scratch. The demo progresses from voice-driven planning through parallel sub-agents working simultaneously on distinct features (confetti animation, image-generated trophies, preset locations) to a full-stack SQLite backend with game history and high scores. The through-line is how an agent-first GUI changes the development loop: the human reviews plans, leaves comments on artifacts, grants or denies shell commands, and fans work across isolated work trees, while agents handle research, code generation, image generation, and browser-based self-verification in parallel.
key_points: Anti-Gravity 2.0 centers on a project primitive that bundles folders and Git repositories as the launchpad for agents, replacing the previous IDE-embedded agent manager with a dedicated orchestration surface.

Gemini 2.5 Flash is the model powering the coding agent throughout the demo; its speed is repeatedly cited as the reason parallel sub-agents can return results before the user finishes reviewing another branch of work.

Work trees are a first-class feature: selecting 'new work tree' spins up a copy of the codebase in a separate folder so a sub-agent can iterate without touching the main conversation, enabling genuinely parallel development without merge conflicts mid-session.

Command permissioning is explicit and granular: the agent asks before running shell commands, and the user can whitelist categories, require per-command approval, or enable sandboxing and network permissioning from settings.

The slash-browser tool lets the agent open Chrome, navigate the running app, and verify its own work visually, closing the loop between code generation and runtime correctness without human intervention.

Adding a SQLite backend with user game history, favorite maps, and high-score tracking is framed as a test of the sub-agent architecture on a gnarly, cross-cutting refactor that touches both client and server.
